**Bopomofo**, also called **Zhuyin Fuhao**[[1]](#cite_note-1) ([/dʒuːˌjɪn fuːˈhaʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*joo-YIN foo-HOW*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); 注音符號; *Zhùyīn fúhào*; 'phonetic symbols'), or simply **Zhuyin**,[[2]](#cite_note-2) is a [transliteration system](/wiki/Chinese_transliteration "Chinese transliteration") for [Standard Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese") and other [Sinitic languages](/wiki/Sinitic_languages "Sinitic languages"). It is the principal method of teaching Mandarin pronunciation in [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"). It consists of 37 characters and five [tone](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)") marks, which together can transcribe all possible sounds in [Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Taiwanese_Mandarin "Taiwanese Mandarin").

Bopomofo was first introduced in China during the 1910s by the [Beiyang government](/wiki/Beiyang_government "Beiyang government"), where it was used alongside [Wade–Giles](/wiki/Wade%E2%80%93Giles "Wade–Giles"), a romanization system which used a modified [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet"). Today, Bopomofo is more common in Taiwan than on the mainland, and is used as the primary [electronic input method](/wiki/Chinese_input_method "Chinese input method") for [Taiwanese Mandarin](/wiki/Taiwanese_Mandarin "Taiwanese Mandarin"), as well as in dictionaries and other non-official documents.

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bopomofo&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

*Bopomofo* is the name used for the system by the [International Organization for Standardization](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization") (ISO) and [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode"). Analogous to how the word *[alphabet](/wiki/Alphabet "Alphabet")* is derived from the names of the first two letters, *[alpha](/wiki/Alpha "Alpha")* and *[beta](/wiki/Beta "Beta")*, the name *bopomofo* derives from the first four syllabographs in the system's conventional consonant order: ㄅ, ㄆ, ㄇ, and ㄈ.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), the system is commonly known by its official name *Zhuyin fuhao* (注音符號; 'phonetic symbols'), or simply as *zhuyin* (注音; 'phonetic notation'). In official documents, it is occasionally called *Mandarin Phonetic Symbols I* (國語注音符號第一式), abbreviated as "MPS I" (注音一式),[[4]](#cite_note-GIO-4)[[5]](#cite_note-headlines-5) to distinguish it from the [Mandarin Phonetic Symbols II](/wiki/Mandarin_Phonetic_Symbols_II "Mandarin Phonetic Symbols II") (MPS II) system published in 1984. Formerly, the system was named *Guoyin zimu* (國音字母; 'national language alphabet') and *Zhuyin zimu* (注音字母; 'phonetic alphabet').[[4]](#cite_note-GIO-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bopomofo&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bopomofo&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Origins")]

Main article: [Commission on the Unification of Pronunciation § Phonetic symbols](/wiki/Commission_on_the_Unification_of_Pronunciation#Phonetic_symbols "Commission on the Unification of Pronunciation")

The [Commission on the Unification of Pronunciation](/wiki/Commission_on_the_Unification_of_Pronunciation "Commission on the Unification of Pronunciation"), led by [Wu Zhihui](/wiki/Wu_Zhihui "Wu Zhihui") from 1912 to 1913, created a system called *Zhuyin Zimu*,[[4]](#cite_note-GIO-4) which was based on [Zhang Binglin](/wiki/Zhang_Binglin "Zhang Binglin")'s shorthand. It was used as the official phonetic script to annotate the sounds of the characters in accordance with the [Old National Pronunciation](/wiki/Old_National_Pronunciation "Old National Pronunciation").[[6]](#cite_note-Dong-6) A draft was released on 11 July 1913, by the Republic of China National Ministry of Education, but it was not officially proclaimed until 23 November 1928.[[4]](#cite_note-GIO-4) It was first named *Guóyīn Zìmǔ* 'national pronunciation alphabet', but in April 1930 was renamed *Zhùyīn Fúhào* 'phonetic symbols' to address fears that the alphabetic system might independently replace [Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_characters "Chinese characters").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Modern use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bopomofo&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Modern use")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/CADAL11100176_%E6%B3%A8%E9%9F%B3%E6%BC%A2%E5%AD%97.djvu/page24-250px-CADAL11100176_%E6%B3%A8%E9%9F%B3%E6%BC%A2%E5%AD%97.djvu.jpg)](/w/index.php?title=File:CADAL11100176_%E6%B3%A8%E9%9F%B3%E6%BC%A2%E5%AD%97.djvu&page=24)

A guide on how to typeset Bopomofo alongside characters. (1936, [Li Jinxi](/wiki/Li_Jinxi "Li Jinxi"))

Bopomofo is the predominant phonetic system in teaching reading and writing in [elementary school](/wiki/Elementary_school "Elementary school") in Taiwan. In elementary school, particularly in the lower years, Chinese characters in textbooks are often annotated with Bopomofo as [ruby characters](/wiki/Ruby_character "Ruby character") as an aid to learning. Additionally, one children's newspaper in Taiwan, the *[Mandarin Daily News](/wiki/Mandarin_Daily_News "Mandarin Daily News")*, annotates all articles with Bopomofo ruby characters.

It is also the most popular way for Taiwanese to enter Chinese characters into computers and [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") and to look up characters in a dictionary.

In teaching Mandarin, Taiwan institutions and some overseas communities, such as Filipino Chinese, use Bopomofo.

Bopomofo is shown in a position secondary to that of [Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin "Hanyu Pinyin") in all editions of *[Xiandai Hanyu Cidian](/wiki/Xiandai_Hanyu_Cidian "Xiandai Hanyu Cidian")* from the 1960 edition to the current 2016 edition (7th edition).

Bopomofo is also used to transcribe other Chinese languages, most commonly [Taiwanese Hokkien](/wiki/Taiwanese_Hokkien "Taiwanese Hokkien") and [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese"); however, its use can be applied to practically any variety in handwriting (because not all letters are encoded). Outside of Chinese, Bopomofo letters are also used in [Hmu](/wiki/Hmu_language "Hmu language") and [Ge](/wiki/Gejia_language "Gejia language") languages by a small number of Hmu Christians.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Bopomofo in Regular, Handwritten Regular & Cursive formats

The Bopomofo characters were created by [Zhang Binglin](/wiki/Zhang_Binglin "Zhang Binglin"), taken mainly from "[regularized](/wiki/Regular_script "Regular script")" forms of ancient Chinese characters, the modern readings of which contain the sound that each letter represents. The consonants are listed in order of [place of articulation](/wiki/Place_of_articulation "Place of articulation"), from the front of the mouth to the back, /b/, /p/, /m/, /f/, /d/, /t/, /n/, /l/, etc.

## Writing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bopomofo&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Writing")]

### Stroke order

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bopomofo&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Stroke order")]

Bopomofo is written in the same [stroke order](/wiki/Stroke_order "Stroke order") rule as Chinese characters. ㄖ is written with three strokes, unlike the character from which it is derived ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 日; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *rì*), which has four strokes.

ㄧ can be written as a vertical line ([![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Bpmf-i2.svg/20px-Bpmf-i2.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bpmf-i2.svg)) or a horizontal line ([![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Bpmf-i.svg/20px-Bpmf-i.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bpmf-i.svg)); both are accepted forms. Traditionally, it should be written as a horizontal line in vertical writing, and a vertical line in horizontal writing. The People's Republic of China almost exclusively uses horizontal writing, so the vertical form (in the rare occasion that Bopomofo is used) has become the standard form there. Language education in Taiwan generally uses vertical writing, so most people learn it as a horizontal line, and use a horizontal form even in horizontal writing. In 2008, the Taiwanese Ministry of Education decided that the primary form should always be the horizontal form, but that the vertical form is an accepted alternative.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Unicode 8.0.0 published an errata in 2014 that updates the representative glyph to be the horizontal form.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Computer fonts may only display one form or the other, or may be able to display both if the font is aware of changes needed for vertical writing.

Bopomofo is occasionally unofficially handwritten as syllable blocks, similar to [Hangul](/wiki/Hangul "Hangul"), however this is not considered an accepted form by the People's Republic of China nor the Republic of China, and it is unsupported by Unicode.

### Tonal marks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bopomofo&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Tonal marks")]

As shown in the following table, [tone](/wiki/Mandarin_phonology#Tones "Mandarin phonology") marks for the second, third, and fourth tones are shared between bopomofo and [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin_tones "Pinyin tones"). In bopomofo, the mark for first tone is usually omitted but can be included,[[19]](#cite_note-bopomofo2017-19)[[20]](#cite_note-bopomofo2017en-20) while a dot above indicates the fifth tone (also known as the [neutral tone](/wiki/Neutral_tone "Neutral tone")). In pinyin, a [macron](/wiki/Macron_(diacritic) "Macron (diacritic)") (overbar) indicates the first tone, and the lack of a marker usually indicates the fifth (light) tone.

| [Tone](/wiki/Tone_number "Tone number") | Bopomofo | | [Pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin") | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Tone Marker | [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") Name | Tone Marker | Unicode Name |
| 1 | ˉ | Modifier Letter Macron (usually omitted)[[19]](#cite_note-bopomofo2017-19)[[20]](#cite_note-bopomofo2017en-20) | ◌̄ | Combining Macron |
| 2 | ˊ | Modifier Letter Acute Accent | ◌́ | Combining Acute Accent |
| 3 | ˇ | Caron | ◌̌ | Combining Caron |
| 4 | ˋ | Modifier Letter Grave Accent | ◌̀ | Combining Grave Accent |
| 5 | ˙ | Dot Above[[21]](#cite_note-21) | · | Middle Dot (usually omitted)[[22]](#cite_note-22) |

Unlike Hanyu Pinyin, Bopomofo aligns well with the Chinese characters in books whose texts are [printed vertically](/wiki/Horizontal_and_vertical_writing_in_East_Asian_scripts "Horizontal and vertical writing in East Asian scripts"), making Bopomofo better suited for annotating the pronunciation of vertically oriented Chinese text.

When used in conjunction with Chinese characters, Bopomofo is typically placed to the right of the Chinese character vertically in both [vertical print](/wiki/Horizontal_and_vertical_writing_in_East_Asian_scripts "Horizontal and vertical writing in East Asian scripts")[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) and [horizontal print](/wiki/Horizontal_and_vertical_writing_in_East_Asian_scripts "Horizontal and vertical writing in East Asian scripts")[[25]](#cite_note-25) or to the top of the Chinese character in horizontal print (see [Ruby characters](/wiki/Ruby_character "Ruby character")).

### Erhua transcription

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bopomofo&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Erhua transcription")]

Words rhotacized as a result of [erhua](/wiki/Erhua "Erhua") are spelled with ㄦ attached to the syllable (like 歌兒（ㄍㄜㄦ） *gēr*). In case the syllable uses other tones than the 1st tone, the tone mark is attached to the penultimate letter standing for syllable nucleus, but not to ㄦ (e.g. 哪兒（ㄋㄚˇㄦ） [*nǎr*](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%93%AA%E5%85%92 "wikt:哪兒"); 一（ㄧ）點兒（ㄉㄧㄢˇㄦ） [*yīdiǎnr*](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B8%80%E9%BB%9E%E5%85%92 "wikt:一點兒"); 好（ㄏㄠˇ）玩兒（ㄨㄢˊㄦ） [*hǎowánr*](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A5%BD%E7%8E%A9%E5%85%92 "wikt:好玩兒")).[[26]](#cite_note-26)

| ㆿ | [ɐ](/wiki/Near-open_central_vowel "Near-open central vowel") | a |

If a syllable ends with a consonant other than *-an* or *-aan*, the consonant's letter is added, then followed by a final middle dot.

-ㄞ is used for [aːi] (*aai*) (e.g. 敗, ㄅㄞ *baai6*, "to be defeated").

-ㄣ is used for [ɐn] (*an*) (e.g. 跟, ㄍㄣ *gan1*, "to follow"), and -ㄢ is used for [aːn] (*aan*) (e.g. 間, ㄍㄢ *gaan1*, "within"). Other vowels that end with *-n* use -ㄋ· for the final ㄋ. (e.g. 見, ㄍㄧㄋ· *gin3*, "to see").

-ㄡ is used for [ɐu] (*au*). (e.g. 牛, ㄫㄡ, *ngau4*, "cow") To transcribe [ou] (*ou*), it is written as ㄛㄨ (e.g. 路, ㄌㄛㄨ *lou6*, "path").

ㄫ is used for both initial *ng-* (as in 牛, ㄫㄡ, *ngau4*) and final *-ng* (as in 用, ㄧㄛㄫ·, *jung6* "to use").

ㄐ is used for [t͡s] (*z*) (e.g. 煑, ㄐㄩ *zyu2*, "to cook") and ㄑ is used for [t͡sʰ] (*c*) (e.g. 全, ㄑㄩㄋ· *cyun4*, "whole").

During the time when Bopomofo was proposed for Cantonese, tones were not marked.

## Computer uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bopomofo&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Computer uses")]

### Input method

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bopomofo&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Input method")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Bopomofo.jpg/250px-Bopomofo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bopomofo.jpg)

An example of a Bopomofo keypad for Taiwan

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Keyboard_layout_Zhuyin.svg/330px-Keyboard_layout_Zhuyin.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Keyboard_layout_Zhuyin.svg)

A typical [keyboard layout](/wiki/Keyboard_layout "Keyboard layout") for Bopomofo on computers

Bopomofo can be used as an [input method for Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_input_methods_for_computers "Chinese input methods for computers"). It is one of the few input methods that can be found on most modern personal computers without having to download or install any additional software. It is also one of the few input methods that can be used for inputting Chinese characters on certain [cell phones](/wiki/Cell_phone "Cell phone").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. On the [QWERTY](/wiki/QWERTY "QWERTY") keyboard, the symbols are ordered column-wise top-down (e.g. `8`+`I`+`K`+`,`)
